frameworks.name: Three-Option Objection Routing
frameworks.definition: When a service objection blocks a client segment, route through three options in order: (1) sell through the objection, (2) redesign delivery to remove the objection, (3) restrict the target audience to those who lack the objection.

claims.text: For LinkedIn agency founders offering content-and-DM services, founders at larger company sizes raise brand-risk objections — they are unwilling to have DMs sent from their personal account — creating a ceiling on client acquisition unless the agency reframes or restructures its offer.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: LinkedIn agency offering DM outreach as a service
Target clients are founder-CEOs above a certain revenue threshold
claims.mechanism: Larger-company founders perceive personal brand damage risk from outsourced DM outreach because their reputation is already an asset worth protecting; smaller or earlier-stage founders have less brand equity to lose and therefore less resistance.
bounded_by: Does not apply when DMs are sent from a branded company account rather than the founder's personal account
Does not apply when targeting early-stage founders with low brand equity

claims.text: For LinkedIn agency founders facing brand-risk objections, the highest-leverage response is to sell the prospect on the direct link between business growth and personal brand strength: a CEO whose business is bigger has a stronger personal brand, making the objection self-defeating.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Client is a founder-CEO whose personal brand is tied to their business success
The objection is brand risk from DM outreach, not a technical or compliance concern
claims.mechanism: A founder-CEO's personal brand value scales with their business success; therefore, growing the business via outbound DMs increases, not decreases, brand strength — reframing the perceived risk as the opposite of the actual outcome.
bounded_by: Does not apply when the founder's brand is deliberately independent of their company (e.g., a celebrity who happens to own a business)
Does not apply when the DM content itself is low-quality or misaligned with the founder's positioning

claims.text: For LinkedIn agencies encountering founder brand-risk objections, a 50% acceptance rate after actively selling the reframe is sufficient to sustain agency growth, because half of the prospect pool converting is an adequate volume.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Agency is actively selling founders on the brand-growth reframe
Not all prospects need to convert — partial conversion is acceptable

numbers.axis: prospect acceptance rate after brand-growth reframe

bounded_by: Assumes sufficient total prospect volume to make 50% conversion meaningful in absolute terms

claims.text: For LinkedIn DM agencies, restricting the target audience to smaller/earlier-stage founders who have not yet developed strong personal brands is a valid market segmentation response to the brand-risk objection — it is a feature of the model, not a flaw, because those clients have lower objection rates.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Agency is unable or unwilling to sell through the brand-risk objection
Smaller founders have lower brand equity and therefore lower risk perception
claims.mechanism: Smaller founders have less established personal brands and therefore perceive lower risk from outsourced DM outreach; targeting them eliminates the objection at the source rather than requiring a reframe.
bounded_by: Creates a ceiling on average deal size since smaller founders have smaller budgets
Does not resolve the growth ceiling if the agency wants to eventually serve larger clients

examples.text: In the conversation, the agency founder describes running a full-service LinkedIn content and DM agency. They report that founder-CEOs above a certain ARR are resistant to having DMs sent from their personal account because of brand risk concerns. The advisor responds by walking through three options: targeting only smaller founders who lack the brand-risk objection, redesigning the offer to work from the company brand instead of the personal account, or increasing DM volume so the math works even with lower conversion. The advisor then argues that the best approach is to sell larger founders on the idea that a bigger business strengthens — not weakens — their personal brand, making the brand-risk objection logically self-defeating.

practices.text: If a service objection is blocking a high-value client segment, first attempt to sell through the objection by reframing it; only fall back to audience restriction or delivery redesign if the reframe fails.

When a LinkedIn DM agency cannot gain personal-account access from a founder, explore whether DMs from the company brand account achieve sufficient volume to make the service viable at a lower per-send conversion rate.

Ask a hesitant founder-CEO: what is your objective as a CEO, and would your brand be stronger if your business were bigger? Use their answer to reframe outbound DM services as brand-building, not brand-risking.
